← Back

Adding an Optional "Hint" Button to a 3D Reconstruction Model

June 22, 2026

In plain words: what is this about

Have you ever scanned a room with your phone and gotten a 3D model you can spin around? The technology behind it is called 3D reconstruction: you give a computer many photos, and it “stitches” those flat photos into a solid space — while also guessing where each photo was taken from, and which way the camera was pointing. That last part is called the camera pose.

Lately there’s a class of very capable AI models (the one here is called Pi3 / Pi3X): you hand it a sequence of photos, and it outputs two things at once — the 3D shape, and the camera pose for every photo. You don’t have to tell it anything extra.

So where’s the problem

When there are many photos (hundreds, thousands), the model can’t swallow them in one bite. The engineering trick is to cut the sequence into chunks, process each chunk, then stitch the chunks back together.

Stitching is where trouble comes from. It’s like copying a long scroll in sections and rejoining them — the seams never line up perfectly, and things start to “drift.” The more chunks, the more drift, until the whole space comes out tilted or wobbly.

What I did

Here’s the key insight: very often, we already roughly know where the camera was. Robot vacuums, cars, drones — they carry positioning sensors that can tell you “I’m roughly here, facing roughly this way.” That information isn’t perfectly precise, but it’s useful.

And this AI model already supports taking such a “roughly where the camera was” hint — the upper layer that handles long sequences just never exposed the entrance. It’s like the car has the feature, but there’s no button for it on the dashboard.

What I did is small: I installed the button. I added an optional switch so you can feed in known camera positions as a hint.

Three design choices that keep it safe

This is the part I find most worth telling, because it’s about restraint:

  1. A hint is only a hint. The model treats your positions as a reference, not as law. So even if your sensor is noisy or off, it won’t drag the result astray.
  2. It doesn’t demand scale. Internally the model makes your positions relative, not dependent on their absolute size. In plain terms: you don’t have to worry whether the units or the scale are right.
  3. Stitching still listens to the model itself. How things finally join, and where the cameras finally land, still come from what the model computed — not from the hint you fed in. The hint helps while thinking, but never oversteps when handing in the answer.

In one line: use it if you have it; if it’s wrong, it’s not fatal.

The result

In the scenes that are “cut into many chunks and drift the most,” adding the hint improved stitching stability by roughly 2 to 2.6×. The longer and more complex the scene, the more it helps; very short scenes barely drift to begin with, so the gain is small.

Why I put this here

This is just a tiny open-source contribution — one Pull Request, a few dozen lines of code. But it’s exactly the kind of thing I want to do:

A powerful tool already exists, but a small barrier was left unconnected. I chipped that barrier down a little, so more people who already hold the right data can use the tool more easily. That’s what I keep saying — to make the future that has already arrived a little more equal for more people. Even if it’s just installing a button.

Links

  • Pull Request #6 · GitHub
